region_of_origin: South India (Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Kerala, Andhra Pradesh)
food_subgroup: Deep-fried legume fritter; made from ground urad dal (split black lentil without skin), shaped into a ring and deep-fried

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Medu Vada or South Indian fried urad dal fritters. Closest parent would be a generic legume-based fried snack. No code assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Legume-based snack food (closest parent: pulse-based fried snack). FoodEx2 A0CKP represents pulse-based products; used as closest defensible parent for urad dal fritter.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Medu Vada. A0CKP (pulse-based snack) used as closest defensible parent anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Medu Vada or equivalent South Indian fried snack preparation.

main_ingredients: urad dal (split black lentil without skin)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Medu Vada is 2 pieces, weighing approximately 120g, as commonly served at South Indian tiffin restaurants and breakfast stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Medu Vada / Urad Dal Fritter
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN — Indian Council of Medical Research, National Institute of Nutrition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods for urad dal-based preparations and deep-fried fritters. Per-100g values adapted to reflect the deep-fried medu vada composition including absorbed frying oil. Core macros (energy 233 kcal, protein 10g, fat 18g, carbohydrate 9g per 100g) are consistent with ICMR-NIN data for deep-fried urad dal preparations. Micronutrients cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for urad dal (NDB 16132) where ICMR-NIN does not report individual micronutrient values.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Urad Dal and Urad Dal-based Preparations
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN data for urad dal preparations
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of ~38 (low) is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entries for urad dal (black gram) preparations. Urad dal has a well-documented low GI due to its high protein and fibre content and intact legume starch structure. Despite deep-frying, the starch gelatinisation is limited and the legume matrix remains largely intact, preserving the low GI. GL per 2-piece serving (120g) calculated as: GI 38 × available carbohydrate 10.8g / 100 = 4.1. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.guidance: Medu Vada contains 336mg sodium per standard 2-piece serving (120g) from salt added to the batter; individuals with hypertension should limit accompaniments such as sambar (which can add a further 200–400mg sodium per bowl) and monitor total meal sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~38) and very low glycemic load (~4.1 per 2-piece serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Medu Vada has a GI of approximately 38 (low) and a GL of approximately 4.1 per 2-piece serving, making it one of the most glycaemically favourable fried Indian snacks for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the intact urad dal legume starch matrix, combined with 12g protein and 3.6g dietary fibre per serving, significantly blunts postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 18g per 100g (21.6g per serving) from deep-frying; saturated fat is low at 3g per 100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Medu Vada is deep-fried and provides 21.6g total fat per 2-piece serving, though saturated fat is relatively low at 3.6g per serving when vegetable oil is used; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume in moderation and prefer preparations using unsaturated vegetable oils, while noting the zero cholesterol and beneficial legume protein profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High iron (3.0mg per serving), good folate (60mcg per serving), zero cholesterol, plant-based protein
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Medu Vada provides 3.0mg iron and 60mcg folate per 2-piece serving, both clinically relevant for rheumatoid arthritis patients (particularly those on methotrexate who require adequate folate); the 100% plant-based, zero-cholesterol profile and predominantly unsaturated fat from vegetable oil are consistent with an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Medu Vada is a classic South Indian deep-fried fritter made from a thick batter of soaked and wet-ground urad dal (split black lentil without skin), seasoned with black peppercorns, cumin seeds, curry leaves, green chilies, and salt. The batter is shaped into a ring (resembling a doughnut) and deep-fried in vegetable oil until the exterior is dark golden and crispy while the interior remains soft and spongy. Two pieces constitute a standard serving of approximately 120g. Per 100g: 233 kcal, 10g protein, 18g fat (saturated fat only 3g), 9g carbohydrate, 3g dietary fibre, 280mg sodium. Despite being deep-fried, Medu Vada has a remarkably low glycemic index of approximately 38 and a glycemic load of only 4.1 per 2-piece serving, because the intact legume starch structure of urad dal is largely preserved during frying. This makes it one of the most glycaemically favourable fried snacks in South Indian cuisine and clinically appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The high protein (12g per serving) and dietary fibre (3.6g per serving) further blunt postprandial glucose response. Iron content is notable at 3.0mg per serving, relevant for anaemia management. Medu Vada is 100% plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is traditionally served with coconut chutney and sambar at South Indian tiffin restaurants. In Singapore, it is widely available at Little India (Serangoon Road) Tamil restaurants, banana leaf restaurants, and Indian tiffin shops such as Komala Vilas and Ananda Bhavan.
common_user_queries: is medu vada good for diabetics

confidence_reason: Core macros and micronutrients sourced from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, the primary approved source for Indian dishes. GI value of ~38 is well-supported by University of Sydney GI Database entries for urad dal preparations. The food is a well-documented, standardised South Indian preparation with consistent nutritional profiles across approved sources.
notes: vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: Medu Vada is a 100% plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally zero in all plant foods and confirmed absent per ICMR-NIN data for urad dal preparations. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero: no animal-derived or UV-exposed ingredients are present. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero: 100% plant-based preparation with no animal fats. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero when vegetable oil is used for frying; trace amounts possible if partially hydrogenated oils are used in commercial preparations. monounsaturated_fat_g is -1.0 — not reported by source. polyunsaturated_fat_g is -1.0 — not reported by source. water_g is -1.0 — not reported by source. Portions: the canonical serving is 2 pieces (~120g); 100g per-unit values are also provided for database use. FoodEx2 code A0CKP used as closest defensible parent (pulse-based snack); no exact code exists for Medu Vada. Ontology gap noted for FoodOn and SNOMED CT. Copper (0.25mg/100g), manganese (0.3mg/100g), and vitamin K (4.5mcg/100g) values are from enriched_json source with confidence 0.9; these are consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for urad dal and are retained as plausible estimates.
